speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund was a teacher in the Troy area for many years. She vividly remembers how hard life was in Sweden as a child. She also describes the trip that her mother and three sisters made over to the United States at length. The preparations, the boat ride, arrival at Castle Garden, and the train ride to Troy. Other memories on this tape include a mean schoolteacher setting a cows leg, her determination to study and to teach.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Boys lived down in the valley below and was very ill and useless. So we took food along and other things and we walked on the railroad track. The train had just gone by and the useless were so poor and that boy was so sick that they couldn't do anything for the pigs and the cows. They had some cows too and they were walking on the railroad track and they never stopped the train or anything for it. They just bled all over in the snow on those little pigs and the mother pig. We ran to look at the cows and they got through together. If they scatter then they always try to get into a group. I had walked on that road before and I knew all those people. As I said, I had the kids in school. Then there one cow had a broken leg between the ankle part here and the knee. It was broken, clear broken, but the skin was still on it and the meat and everything like that. We just looked at each other.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: What were we to do? Were we to leave that cow there like that and get the food to useless? Or are we going to take the cow first?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: But then when Harriet was smarter than I was, she said, we better take the cow first because we can't get the cow down unless we do something. But we couldn't do something either. I don't know how. We must have gone a quarter of a mile and a way down to the house with that cow and that leg. She'd stopped the sweat, just pulled from her, that old cow. We got down there and they were just shocked, all of them. Then I took over because Harriet says, Ann, I can't do a thing. I don't know what to do. I said, let's get her into the barn. The barn was clean and it was a low kind of a shacky thing, but it was fairly nice. We'll trip her, pull off the other leg, the sound, and pull that from under her. Then the broken one would be on the top where we could work. We tied the cow's head to the manger so she couldn't move.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: What is the thing that you make fence with?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Fence, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: A wire?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: A common ordinary wire.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: You tighten the wire with a what? Isn't it terrible?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I know what you're talking about. It's what you tighten the fence wire with. Yes, yes. We had to put that clear outside of the barn because we didn't have any much in ropes and things like that either. We got her across like that. There were four or five kids, you know. I said, go out and get moss. It was late in the fall, I think it was. No, it was winter because I can remember that bloodbath track.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: We made the kids put all kinds of soft boughs and things like that underneath her. Then we tied her at the manger and then we tightened the wire tightener. That's what it was. You know how they do that. They had the wheels on them. You turn it and tighten it. Well, yeah. This is, yes, on a fence. You have to tighten the rope like this, you know. It had several wheels, you know, that goes in. And I can't talk English.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Then we pulled on that, had the kids pull. And the mother was in the house because he was so sick and feverish and we didn't know when he was going to fall out of bed too. So she ran back. But the kids, they're fairly good size, 12 or 13 years old, I guess. We kept on pulling slowly and slowly and I worked on either side of the break, you know. And I could just tell how it had slipped out like that, you know. This is the way the leg was. And when it broke, it went like that, you know. She couldn't possibly have gotten very far, you know, with a leg like that. And then we slowly, slowly, slowly pulled until we got them even like that. And then we, in the meantime, they had gotten splints from shingles or it was a shingle block or it was shingles are thin, of course. We made them out of the block of cedarwood. And you know, the leg is like that on a cow, you know, the bend of it, you know. And after we got them so they just fit like that, then we put the moss and rags across that. And then we had them in such a way that if the cow stepped on the foot, the end of it would go into this crouch here, you see. See, you know how the back of leg is, you know. And we put it, it's all right if they did stick into the flesh too because it had to be done. And so we spliced them like that and took some canvas that we found outside and we had nails but they were on the outside so they wouldn't hurt you, you know. And there she lay and carried water in a big dishpan, in a dishpan they had and the only thing to carry in was a dishpan. And then the school after, the next day and the next day kids took turns about, you know, to help me. And finally the day came when, it must have been, I don't know, school was out. It must have been spring then when the snow was still on the ground. That's what it was because I had to go to summer school, you know, just as soon as school was out.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And I wrote to them and the boy died.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: My brother -in -law was a doctor, a doctor here in Troy for many years and he told me what to tell them to do because he had plenty of things here.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: And I remember there were a lot of stories when they had, what was it, what disease was it that got all over the country? Was it typhoid?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: No.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Cholera?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: No, it's... They had the flu epidemic. Well, it started with... Scarlet fever too. Scarlet fever was bad too. Yeah, but that, what you said was the start of it. Well, I'll probably see it in some paper.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Influenza? Influenza?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, that's the same thing as flu. No, anyway, he told us what to do and as long as we were home.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: But when I had to go to school and Harriet had to go to school or did she go home to Iowa for that summer?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I don't know. So it was in the spring. And the years went by and the boy died for, of course, that same week. And he had... Oh, they blowed up so terribly. He was like a balloon. And we couldn't get any doctors, no telephone. And so we walked out, of course, and got to Derry to get to a telephone. But no doctors was to be found around there. Well, anyway, he died. And he would have died whether we'd been over there or not, of course. It was terrible. And the years went by and then one day I was going to Seattle, not too many years ago, maybe it's eight, nine, ten years ago.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And this was 40 years ago or more, don't you see?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And I went to... I don't know why, I went up to the cashier or something like that. I wanted to change some money or something. I was going to take... From the airplane to the train to make better... I guess Alex was sick. I guess that was the reason I went to Seattle that day. And then there at the window, I said, well, I can't remember her first name now.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I said, should I know you by your Anna Marie?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And we hadn't for years seen them and then they came out. They came out in the summer, sister to two of them. And we went to find out where the old folks were, but they were dead. But they wanted to see the place and the Lawrence's. They knew where the house had stood and everything. We used to go all the way down there to the small river. Same thing that... Moonman Dylan put Mrs. Gary on that log. But they had cut up, you know, they had train railroad up there then, you know, when we were there. And so we went...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What happened to that cow? Did the cow survive?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: She survived. She survived. And the only reason why we would have not done anything to it would have been we would have slaughtered because it needed the meat. But the small children had to have milk. And the cow lay there until almost rotted on one side. But they were so good to the cow and what most from the trees, mostly I think I said grass too. And that's why there's some grass sticking up in there, you know, that you can dry by the creeks around there. Well, anyway, it's...

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Life can be very adventuresome, can't it be?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And everyone in Helmer said, don't you go, don't you go, you're crazy. Well, we'll take care of those people. They were nice people. And the girls are working... Neither one of them are married, I think. And they remembered that episode, you know, that happened and everything. People are funny. They just... Especially when there is a fight in a big street and guns come out and knives come out and everything like that, they walk as if they've never seen it so as to not be in it. Lots of people like that. They rather take another street. That's something that I've read about them. I haven't seen it.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Could you... I think I could have a hundred if I... But it's so far back now that...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Could you tell us about what it was like in Sweden when you were a little girl before you came over?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, I can tell you. And I can tell a lot. But I started to do that one time just because I was exuberant over something, you know, maybe because I was bragging about Sweden or maybe it was something else. I don't know what I did. Because my sister stepped on my foot and that meant you keep still the best time. That's what it meant, you know. So I don't know if... But I can tell it to you. You don't have to make use of it right now. I'm not going to say so.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: All right?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I was born in 1891. And in 1892, the end of that year, summer, late summer, my father went to America to find a better life for all of us. It was hard all over. And he thought he'd come. Well, it happened to be that terrible summer when it rained so terrible here in this part of the country and around Genesee and Moscow and all over. It rained and it rained. And they had put up the little bit of grain that they had. They stacked them in bundles, you know, and laid them like that, like a sugar cone, you know, like that. And then they did have some machines, some threshing machines. And when they got to a place and it was raining and they couldn't do anything about it, the owner would have to feed them until it dried up. And in one place, I knew the name of them too, because this man taught an old, old man then when I talked to this man. Anyway, the stacks were green like Christmas trees. It was growing, you know, right in the stacks, you know. Big, tall ones about twice the height of a man and bigger than this room. And they put the bundles with tassels in, you know, the wheat in like that and had them slanting. So if it rained, it'd follow the straws, you see, and it wouldn't rot the rain. But it was such a long time. Over a month, I think, before they were able to do it. And people, they molded for some people and they didn't have any things to cover them with. And gunnysacks, it was an awful thing. And in this particular place in Genesee, they killed a cow and two pigs before they got rid of those men, because they had to eat, you know, because that was the law of the land. And my father came in 1891, and in 1893 was, no, my father came in 92, and that terrible rain was in 93. And he didn't have any money. His uncle, his uncle had come here a year or two before, and he had put up a house which was still standing until just a few years ago, right over the hill, because I bought the place just as to get that place after an hour of storms. He was a soldier, and he, my father, I'll go back to him, in order to get food, a fellow by the name of John Johansson, putting up a mill. And as soon as they put up to get some of the wheels going, why they began to saw, because they had to have lumber, because for houses and everything, and shelter for people and animals. And he worked for Pear Johansson, and Pear Johansson traded some boards to my father for his work, and father took those boards and traded them for food, to where he was. Where he was living, you know, there was a family there that took him in, you know, and he got boards from Pear Johansson, and the boards my father gave to this family, so that they could build something in to protect themselves. That was during the hard days. Well then, that was 1992, and then 1993 was an awful year, and 1994,

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: in the late part of summer, my father had worked enough to get a ticket back to Sweden. He had, that last year then, you see, he couldn't, you know, in those first years, but the last year, they were building all over, and including this Uncle Storm, you know, he had a little cabin for himself, just over the hill from where we lived. And then, then my mother got ill. She tried to work, she tried to do so many things, and he knew it, he was afraid that he'd lose her, but he figured that those are golden things in the United States, you know, it would be such a wonderful thing to make money and get back there, you see, that's what he was trying to do. And he came home then in, in 19, 1896, so he was there in 1996, his course on four, I figured out long ago that it was four years between each time when he went and came. So, he went home, and mother was ill, and had taken her to a neighbor that took care of her, and when Anne, this storm that was here in America, he left his wife and three children, and this woman, my aunt, was a very religious woman. All children, when they were baptized, they had a godmother to nurse a child if anything should happen to it, and the mother died or something like that. So, this storm, she took me, and a neighbor some distance away from us who was indirectly, well,

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: acquainted and friends to my mother, they took her. She was three or four years older, I think it was, and then another relative took the third one of us, so there were three little girls, and they had promised to God that they would nurture and care for the children if something should happen to the mother, too. Well, he came home, they wrote to my father, and he came home, and immediately got a home. We did have a home, but it wasn't anything to do with it. And he went to this home, he put a payment down on that, and then he went to work. He was a woodsman, you know, and a carpenter. And he stayed, he got to the United States in 92, and at 96 he went back to Sweden and stayed there for four years, and then went back to the United States again. And mother still was not well. It was too much for her what she had done, her wood and her snow and everything like that. It was the same all over. And that 1993, 1893 was the same in Europe as it was here in America. And then I was,

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: well, we had another sister after father got back to the United States. She was in four years, you know, between each number of years. So there was Signe. She was eight years old. And my sister Esther was ten, and I was twelve, and my sister Olivia was sixteen. That's the way it came out. And she was working in a factory in Sweden, and I wanted to, I've been confirmed, no I wasn't confirmed, but I was through with the religious part of it and things like that. And we, mother called us together. It was a letter from dad, and he said he wanted us to come. Oh, Olivia was sixteen, and lots of fun in Sweden. And to do the kind of work, she was at a paper factory and red sheets of paper and blue sheets of paper. I can remember that. I would have liked to have them to make a dress of it if it had been cloth. And then he wrote again, and couldn't decide. And I think on the third time he says, now he says, you come, or I'll have to come and get you, and that would be lots more expensive. All right.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Mother called her children, little flock together, and she said, do you want to go?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: She said to Olivia, yes. No, no, she said, no, I don't want to go because life's just beginning, you know, for, you get good jobs and things like that in Sweden too. And Esther, all her life, she has cried. Oh, yeah, she didn't want to go either. And then Signe, she just beguided, and she didn't know much what it was all about at that age. And I was the last one there, she asked, and I said, yeah, I'm going. Oh, and everybody cried, and I cried too. And, you know, if you have lived in a country born there and lived there for generations, you know, it's kind of something that draws you, that holds you. You can improve your old country, as they called it, just as well as you improve it in a new country. And it got to be more and more machinery, and I can remember when I saw the first binder in Sweden.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Oh, what was the name of it now?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Oh, let me get it there. Anyway, we were through school then in the spring, and I, we sold what we had, but there wasn't enough tickets for all of us, but Father had sent enough money to go. And, oh, it was exciting to think of it, and I'll be back. I had a sweetheart. He was 12, and I was 12. And we went on the other side of the station, and he bought a great big sack of candy, and they didn't have bags to put candy in in those days. They'd take them.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Oh, there's so many papers here.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: They called candy caramellar. You know, there's one something similar to that in English. Caramel. Caramel. Caramels. That's the only kind they had, but different color, you know. And then, like I saw, and I had a great big one, and it took some, and he says, I don't want you to go. And I said, I don't want to go either. And he said, let's run away. 12 years.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, well, I don't think that dad's a more campy, and that dad's a more campy than tried.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I'd laughed many a time when I thought about it. Well, but he was more true to me than I was to him. I came to a foreign country, and it was just one country. Wonderful. And the last I was teaching in Helmer, when I got the last letter from him.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Before you tell about coming over, would you tell about the fish cleaning in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Yeah, I can tell you. That was when we were so poor, you see, and my father was gone, you know, and my mother was ill. And then they had a distance about, oh, half a mile or something like that away from us. And there were people that lived kind of high in the hills and mountains, east of us, and quite a distance, too, from Hudiksval, which is Hudiksval was the biggest city in that part of the country. And so, when fishing season came, the fishermen would catch fish, and the men that worked, well, they were patches of ground, too, you know, of soil. They took their horse, they had one horse and a cart, two wheeled cart, you see, like a big wheel, like this, and then would be a box on it, like that. And then would put them into shafts, you know, one horse, you know, a shaft. And so they would drive like that from the hills and go to Hudiksval, and then they had to wait sometimes for the fish to come in, and they did what they loaded it, loaded it into those carts, you know. And there were three or four or five men who went in almost like a funeral procession and openly went home. But there was a law that you couldn't clean fish in or near Hudiksval, a city, because of flies and smell and things like that. They had to go farther away out into the country, and it didn't matter if it smelled out in the country, I guess, but around the city they couldn't have anything like that. Well, as I said, Mother was sick, and my sister Esther went with me, but she didn't do anything. She was trying to put away the mosquitoes all night when we were cleaning the fish, and Olivia didn't do anything like that. She was having night shifts, I think, at the factory. I don't know how that was. But anyway, it was Esther and I that were there, and those fishermen would come, those sunnies up just about all night in northern Sweden. And this is exactly northern, but it has Copenhagen and those places in Denmark and southern part of Norway. Anyway, we had gunny sack aprons, and they would stand on your knees, and the man comes and says, I'm going to take a fourth of a barrel.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: How many carrying are there, do you want? You'd say, oh, one, if it's a younger, and no more?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, I'd take two, somebody else said, and those women, you know, possibly take four. And I thought, oh my gosh, I bet I can do that. While they were talking, I was practicing. I had never practiced before. You stand on your knees, and you take a fish like this, and you cut out, take the fingers from this hand, and tear up the entrails, and put the entrails here, and the fish there on the grass, the green, nice green grass, by a little, small lake. Very small. And so you sit like that all night, but you couldn't, a fourth of a barrel is pretty big, even at that, to pick up every fish, you know. And I didn't have any stopping zone. It was summer time, you know. I stood on my knees, and there were other kids there, too. And then the scales from the fish, and come up, if I moved, you know, come up under my skirts, and I tried to have that gunny sack, apron, you know, to keep it down.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And the only thing that Esther would do, we tried to chase it, the, now what was it I said?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Mosquitoes. Mosquitoes. Mosquitoes. That's about the only thing she could do. But I was determined I was going to do what the women did, you see. And finally, it's getting to morning, you see. And I was tired, oh gosh. And then a woman came up, and she said something about God wouldn't be pleased if you misused your body like that or something. She said something, and I was shocked to think that I did that just to get food, you know.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, and how much do you suppose I got?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: One dime. One dime.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I went home to Mother, and I had clasped that dime so tight in my hand. The rest of the kids were walking to me around me, and they were in the same shape, all of them. And we just couldn't get the hand, the bones straightened out. You finally got it, and there was that dime. Of course, I gave it to Mother. But I learned to work fast, you know, they just go like this, you know, like knitting or stocking or something. It's all right. Nobody complained. And that's the thing that I am dissatisfied with in America. Most people are dissatisfied, always dissatisfied. Instead of just doing it and listening and learning, they have to complain, oh, but if it had been for so and so and so and so and he's just a skunk anyway and lazy, they wouldn't do anything. And talk about the... and instead of going out and work, do something.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Why do you think it was so...

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: times were so hard in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, it's the same in Norway, just exactly. And in Denmark and here in this country, they were few and far between. In 1892, there has been lots of people coming, but they were going westward, westward, westward all the time. Oh, I saw... I've seen the women here. Well, I've pitched hay all my life at home here in this country. I've been behind the plow. I've driven the horses in the field, but we got a nice home too, I tell you. But the girls would do the same thing. And my children have done the same thing. They want to. They want to. And they got through, all of them. Now, my sisters, Esther and Olivia, are both graduate nurses. And I had my college training at Washington State College and some at U of I and all kinds of correspondence courses. And I don't... heavenly help me, I don't know what all it was in order to get through. And I had always had a dream that I was going to teach, you know. First day I went to school, I had... before that, I had all kinds of rag dolls. I got to be... so I could handle them pretty good too. And then my sister Signe was just little then, you see, was sitting on the floor and came home. And I put Signe... she got a little rod and she put Signe there. You sit there. And that was I now then saying there. And each one shook a finger at him, so I realized that afterwards when I got older, you know, how really she was... I was aping the teacher. So I had to make the mind. We went to school every other day. You went to school one day to get... to be taught and to have lessons assigned to you. And you go home and you work all day long to do what the teacher has said to do and go through it and have your books and everything. And I don't know but what is a good idea.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: The expense of schooling today is terrible. Just awful. Have you... I think I told you something about him the other day, Greg Mountquist. Greg, I know him. You have known him. Yes. Yeah. He's a fine kid. He is so willing to do anything and everything. He has a girlfriend of course.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Can I ask you one more story about Sweden?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Yeah. The story about getting licorice.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that or was it a fern? Was it fern? Licorice fern or fern?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: It was ferns. Getting ferns. You told us about getting ferns in here. Oh, oh, oh yes. Yes. Right behind our house. Here was a rich man's house over there and I was related to them too. And here was another relative and here was a house here. And then a road went above the hillside there and there was cliffs. That's Glacial Drift in Sweden. You can study that by just studying the hillsides almost. And there are springs all over. In through the rocks, in between the layers it comes out. Maybe it's only as high as this or twice as high as this.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And then all kinds of ferns, especially this, oh, what was it?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: My mother had a Latin name for it. I can't remember now. Anyway, we called it sweet fern too because the roots were sweet. Sweet like sugar. And the little ferns only went about a little bit better than any.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Foot?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Foot. Just about that. And here is another cluster and here is another cluster and there is one that is bigger and you have to be careful because that water trickles and runs all year round except when it's frozen in wintertime and it's all snow on it. And it's just slimy, you know, to get, you've seen that in lots of places in creeks you walk on it and get so slimy kind of. And I can remember how I'd, like a claw, I tried to get into the rock, you know, and climb. And then you have to go easy so you don't break the root of it. It's like a carrot almost or parsnip, only it's crooked roots on it, you know. But the food part of it is in that stem. And then we chew that instead of sugar. And you can boil it and get the sugar out of it, you know, sweeten something. But it takes an awful long time to do all those things. And then little by little they're shipped into Sweden and they got sugar and they got sugar in Russia and they got sugar in Denmark and it's the same all over.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about the trip over? And what do you remember about the trip over to the United States and then the trip out to here?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, my father sent money for the tickets and for some clothes. I suppose he didn't want to see, want the people to see that we came in patch clothes, I don't know. And we took all the things that we possibly could. For an example, my mother's wedding dress and that has got ruined. Alex took it and they were having some kind of a day up here at the schoolhouse and they had to dress up in crazy ways. And if he didn't go upstairs and take mothers, my grandmothers would be dressed and pleated all around but black. At that time they dressed the style to have black wedding dresses with white tulle around the neck, you know, and the sleeves and so on like that. The very last year that we were in Sweden, they had a double wedding from some wealthy home or two homes must have been and two brides, you know, two couples got married and they had white dresses. And we thought that was the most wonderful thing in the world to have white dresses but anyway to go back to what we wore, it wasn't much that we had with us to take. And oh, I have a little, oh my goodness, I can't see why in the world those kids are mine. They're so crazy after those things that, well, I have a copper coffee pot and I have lots of things. This one is head made but not in Sweden.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: So you had just a few things?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: You took as much as you could. Yes, and I had, Esther and I each had a bundle of clothing and bedding because he thought they didn't have any bedding in America, I guess, or something like that, I don't know. But we had and had strapped those bundles and my little sister had a wooden butter.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: A churn?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, not the churn, that's a churn.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: The molds?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, they're small wooden dishes just like they used to have wooden buckets. It's a small bucket with a lid on it and put a little plug of wood key kind of to keep the lid on. And that was very nice and it is in, it will be in Moscow. Now, when I got sick I just parted out everything. I think I had about over 40 China cups, you know, that was a fad a few years ago, you know, of getting all kinds of cups. I'll show you after a while. I have one, one of the Malali girls, he won, only won on my birthday. Then I had, she carried that one and Esther and I carried the bundles and Olivia carried a big suitcase. She had the worst of it but she was the strongest too, you know. And mother had something too in the way of a suitcase. And we ate a fine dinner with the neighbor's house when we left and hauled us to the station and the station very much like the station's here. And we were to go a little bit farther north and across the, the, the Julin Mountain between Norway and Sweden and went down into Christian sand. They called it, called it and had another name for it while there, if you remember. And I remember but I can't say it. I don't, I don't want to fool with it because, just take up your time.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Then we unloaded everything and we went to Kesselgarden at, what's the name of the aisle that I said?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Kessel. Oh, I couldn't talk that so many years. Kessel, Kesselgarden is almost like being in jail, you know, at the Kesselgarden. Iron spokes in, you know, and divide them when, when we had to sign out and sign out and sign out and then we got word that we couldn't leave before two days and then we had, didn't have any food. And, but it, they changed it. Somebody fixed, done something about the trains, you know, and everything going westward.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And they, did I show you this?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I think I haven't got to show you anything. No.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: The first day, and I, I just can't tell you before a gentleman, but I will, that my mother said, now you better go to, go to the outhouse before you get on the ship.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, we watched where others went, in one direction, the women in another one. And, it was an awful space that I have ever seen in my life. And mother was the last one to go. And she said, you better pray to God that you don't get to a country that looks like that. She was so shocked, you know, and we all were terrible. Well, we got to Castle Garden. They unloaded us. And, it was just a kind of fine, too, in a way, educational in a way, because we had, as long as we were on the ship, the ticket included the food, you know, on the ship. And, we were the same way at Castle Garden, on Ellis Island. Now there the word came. And, but when we got on the train, it's a good thing that we had. Mother must have carried that one most of the time. Because, I know how we, I'm pretty sure. And, that little thing, you know, that I said, that keep, like this, but smaller, little bucket, wooden bucket. And, that was filled with butter before we left, you know, because we didn't think it was any good butter in America. So, they had, and they had, we didn't only have that with some bread, if we had on the left, we traded a little bit butter, a tablespoon full of butter, you know, to somebody else that sat near to us and didn't have any butter. Anything or something in the train. But then, when it came, they had to stay overnight, Castle Garden, and they slept in swings, you know, nets, you know, nets swings, you know, one person in each one, you know. And, children cried and were tired and sick and mother string hair, you know, and, and, and, and, and, and, and, and, and, oh, it was terrible. And, then, we finally were checked out. And, when they were, when they were sorting out everything and, and people who they were, and they show the papers and everything like that, then one man, oh, he was a nice man, he was, there was four small children. And, he was quite old. He was an uncle to them. And, he had been to America and had purposely gone to Sweden to, to bring his sister and, and the children to America. And, the father was here to, to work until they got here, you know. And, we thought they were nice and they were. And, we switched when we had to go to get out of those chicken coops that we were in. They dragged this man back and wouldn't let him go. They said he had, there were marks on TB. He couldn't go to America. And, there she was left just, had never figured out anything about America's own, her children. And, she's left a fairly nice home in Sweden. And, I, I don't know how it turned out. I wish, often, if I had found out something about it later, but I didn't. Oh, things like that all the time, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Was the trip over in the boat, was that uncomfortable at all?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Oh, well, at first, it, it wasn't. It was exciting. And, we sang and they danced and everything like that. But then, a storm came up, a terrible storm. And, they pushed the kids down in the holes like that, you know, without trying to find out which partition that they were in, were to be in or anything like that. And, buckle it down and because the water goes over the ship, you see, the deck, you know, the top deck. And, most of them came stearage, you see. It, it was awful. But then, pretty soon, the sun shone and, and we getting closer and closer all the time and we figured, you know, the one hour and days and, and then, it was night. Not, not midnight, it was before midnight, but we were out of the, the, the, the sun didn't shine all the time when we were in the ocean. We were too far south for that, you know. And, then, the, we was looking and looking and looking at kids all around, you know. I was making dog, dog clothes, I can remember. And, the world of what I did that for, I, I'd had it for a long time just to, for the souvenir, I guess. And, someone called, oh, there's a light, a light, a light.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, then, it got a little bit lighter and lighter, you know, when you see that, you have gone through New York, haven't you?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, you know how it is, when, and, and special to have children with you. And, then, that Statue of Liberty. They fell on their knees and thanked God that they, they had, who had helped us to, to get over. It isn't like when somebody goes with just for, to see, because they want to see. And, they stood a tall fellow by me, I tried and tried to, to see, you know. And, he lifted me up. And, then, he set me down and he put his hand on my shoulder. And, he said, little girl. And, then, he, he spoke Swedish. I didn't know he had been to Sweden several times. And, he, he was a Swede, tall, handsome guy. And, he said, be thankful, always be thankful for the land that you are coming to. Build it up. Build it up. Do nothing wrong against it. Oh, he gave me a real sermon by myself. And, then, he quoted in Swedish, give me your tired, your poor, your great messes longing to be free. That's at the foot of the Statue of Liberty, you know. I only quoted some of it. And, I think this is a wonderful country that we're in. Wonderful. Well, we, we got, we got to New York. And, that's where we had so much trouble because, not for us, but that those people, other people too. And, it clicked. And, that, and we had this food with food. They feel that. And, I didn't know what was in it. And, Mother was trying to sleep. And, and, I don't know. Anyway, I, I wanted to, I was listening. Listening. I was asleep. It's too, and sleeping. And, then, pretty soon we're going to get there. I thought, oh, it's a pretty soon we're going to get there. Pretty soon we're going to get there. And, then, we begin to climb the rock, as you know. And, and, so help me if I had been a musician and, and, and a singer, I would have done something out of that because I think I can, I think I can, I think I can, I think I can, I think I can, I think I can. And, then, we go down. I thought I could, I thought I could, I thought I could. And, there are thoughts about this country, how great it is. Well, we went. We got out of food. They're making a difference. And, then, we got to Spuker. And, Mother said, said, I don't know whether she said the dime that I first gave her or something, but anyway, she gave me a dime.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: She said, Did you say you wanted to have something, buy something for that dime?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Yes, I said I do. So I went by myself. I went out.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I thought the conductor said that be ready to board the train at 8 o 'clock. Clock, clock, clock, clock, is Swedish, you know, I knew that, and many other words too.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And then I looked in the window and I thought, what am I going to do with this dime?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And I thought if I could only find a little book, I had the idea in my head that if a book were small, I would be more easy to read. And it's not pamphlets, you know. And I asked how much that was.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And he looked at me and he said, What are you going to use it for?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, I want that little book. If this is enough, well, I think you can get it for less than five cents, he said maybe. And then he said, Well, keep that five, he said.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And then what are you going to do with the five now?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, I said, Mother is sick and she can't do anything about it. And I thought if we could get a few cherries, each one of us gave me a bag of cherries. Then I was on top of the world, I tell you. Then on to the train again, you know, so Spokane. And oh, we fixed our hair, you know, and we fixed up in every way and checked the baggage and everything so it wouldn't lose anything. And by the way, when we crossed the plains, we had hats, mind you. We got hats because we're going to America. And we had them on a shelf above us, you know, likely to have all kinds of things. And those tornadoes things that twist us, you know, on the plains, you know, Kansas and Nebraska and those places. And all the hats but one went out through that, through that. Well, mother didn't have hers on. The girls, I don't know, well, maybe it's only three that went through. But I didn't make a difference. We're getting home, getting home. First home that we've ever had, it's been like anything now. Going home, we're going to the United States. Oh, my, my, my. And then right out here. And there stood my father at the bottom of the steps. And mother started out first and, and Signe, my sister, eight year sister, seven, I guess she was. Somehow I kicked her because I jumped right in my father's arms.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And then a few feet away from it against the, the, the, what is it for the Traysville by down here?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Depot. Depot. And there stood the Uncle Store.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Oh, gray haired, you know, white, you know, and holding out his arms too. And then we walked to the hotel, the same hotel that is there now. And they had, Thompson was the name of the man that owned that hotel at that time. And they had a special dinner for us because Gabe, my father, has told him that his family was coming. And we didn't get in before about two o 'clock in the afternoon on a Sunday. And oh, my, the food, the meat, all those, in those days they had platters for each. They don't have, they didn't have plates for ordinary plates, but they had the meat part was just like it is now when we have the, to pass a meat platter, you know.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And then how far is it to, to, to go?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Oh, just about another hour now, maybe, driving and, and there was a man here. He was only about 10, 12 years old. And father got the livery barn, you know, hired a rig at the livery barn that boy drove, you know. And I can't, can't find him. I can't, I'm not so sure of the name or anything. Anyway, he dumped us and went back and then we were home.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: What do you suppose we did?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, we cried for one thing and we thank God for another one. And I've always done that. And father has gotten some, I have that plate here that belongs to my father. You sit down, this is my chair. There's a reason for it. Two room that he had finished. He was a carpenter. He built, he built, built it of logs.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Haven't I told you this?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: He built it of logs and had a broad axe. That's one of the first things he bought. A broad axe, you know, you hew them, you can walk on the tights and things like that, and hew them like that. Not long ago, you know, that all the tights were hewed with broad axes like that here and all over. And he scored,

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: you score the log, you cut it a V in it like this. So the next log can, you put that, that you score it on one side only, and then you put the other one unscored on top. So it fits like this, you see. And then they have, not brace and bits, but the bigger ones that you can, you can bore a hole in through both of them. Here are the two logs now. Here's one log, and here is another log. And then you have a wooden peg about that wide, a couple of inches. And the hole is just a little bit, it has to fit, of course. And then into the other one like that. So all over our building, our pegs like that, put together by pegs, no nails. Wasn't a single nail when we came.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: It was all like that, with the exception of, let's see now, what was that now?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, the corners. He had, it's one of the ex and a Swede who lived here, he died several years ago, but he, this,

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: old Thomas, close to the cemetery, owns a place now that he made for himself, this man. And he, the rest of them have followed suit. And the corners, they prayed that the time would come that it slipped. So father and that man had a brace, had a, went, had a place to make, to work with metal, you know, heavy metal, plowish hair or something had to be fixed, you know, and harness, go to pieces and so on, like that. Then they go to Sven Eriksen to fix it. And so the corner of that house is a thing like this. And then there's nails on the side, iron nails, but they were square nails, you see. And they're going to stay there as long as I stay around here. Oh, I tell you, I, I'm sick because I ever came here to this cabin.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: What did you think, what did you think of the, of the cabin when you got there, of the homestead?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: You can't imagine, for people that haven't had anything, you know, and come to a place like that and the smell of cedars so nicely, you know. And father had, had, he had varnished the woodwork. And instead of having paper of some kind on the walls, he had oil cloth, you know, and putting the strips like that. This was artistic too, you know, it was nice. And then this time went on, why we had the whole room 16 by something, the, of the one end of the house, it was never finished. The openings for the board, for the windows was, was there a long time, didn't board over, you know, and then slept in the, and in one room upstairs was finished shortly after we got home, got there. And then we had the bed in the kitchen and we had a bed in the, in the bedroom, but the bedroom was, was finished, you see. So we got along very well and it didn't take long before the, the, the, the, began to go to work, you know. We were going to school, Olivia was 16, and she, father told her that I fed you long enough, now you go on and, and earn your own living. He, he is, he's a wonderful, was a wonderful man, but by, you don't fool with men like that. He, he thought that he, she should and she did too. And just as soon as she could, why, the two of them, you know, trained for nurses for three years, four or whatever, like that, Great Falls Montana is quite an institution there. I like to go there sometimes. I had been there once,

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Catholic, and

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: the years went by. We walked to Spring Valley School, four miles, and had, here is the bucket that we carried, you know, somebody, my father didn't smoke tobacco, but every Saturday morning he went to Troy, drove to Troy. He shaved and he bathed and he dressed up in work clothes, of course. I don't have anything bad. He went to Troy and, and played pool with the Driscoll boys.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Why is a good sport too?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: The older I got, the more I appreciated him too, because he, he worked I tell you. Well, anyway, we landed in Troy, the third of August. And my father and another Swede from the locality from which we had come, were working on a church in Derry, the Derry church. They moved it since. And, and he said, now he said you're going to be alone tomorrow because I, I have to finish the tower on that church and clean up the things and so on. So it wouldn't take long, but he'll be there a few days.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And this organ has been in that church.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, and, and, uh, let me see now. He said to mother, now you see too that they, they work. There were thistle, he has planted, had planted a garden. And thistles had grown up all along them and a Scandinavian skin is very thin in a country like that. This talk about a peach, a glow or something like that, but it's thin and, and you easily tear the skin, you know, because they are not used to such a much, so much wind like this and, and so much heat in, in summertime here, you know. So he told, he told him, but look out, but don't, don't put something on your head so that you won't get burnt. Oh, we didn't have anything to put on.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, uh, see now then, uh, Esther and I went to Spring Valley three years. And, uh, the teacher that we had, the first one that we had, John Ogden, the first two years, and he was a terrible thing. If, if a boy snickered or, or, or he looked back so he saw it, he had to pocket, pocket knocking, he threw it and pinned it. He was, had to do working on a piece of paper, you know, and he pinned that paper to the desk.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Should a man be, beheaded for that?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Scared him, scared him all. Well, anyway, when that was over, and during that time, uh, father couldn't have accumulated much money, you know, couldn't, you know, because he was still in trading things in this country then too.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, uh, he, um, see now how was that now?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Uh, I, I, I've skipped. I, uh, I read every night and I had a telekindle. We had made telekindles till, until just lately, and soap and all those things, you know. It was very little that we bought because he got a cow and the calf was a male calf and so butchered him. And then we got a pig, and those days why they aren't, weren't so very much. I think somebody got one for a dollar, a baby pig, you know, that was just weaned. But, uh, as time went on, I, I, as I said, I, I wanted to study all the time. And the first book when I got into the Spring Valley School I had read, you, now that, that, uh, book is very common one. Now what, what would you, what would the name of a book on a shelf in a schoolhouse of a conglomeration of, uh, people, Norwegians and Swedes and Irish and from the Missouri and all over.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Now what kind of a book would, would you, would you want to read?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Especially if you read it before.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Uncle Tom's Cabin. I had, I'd read that in Swedish, you know. It's translated into many languages. Most good books are, you know. And, uh, so there was Uncle Tom's Cabin. And I, I read, and I, I could translate it so quickly to the others because, you see, I had the book, Uncle Tom's Cabin, you see. And, uh, there is a word here and there that is exactly the same, like, uh, we say book, B -O -O -K in Sweden, B -O -K book. B -O -K book, B -O -O -K book. And water, vatem, V -A -T -E -N -W -A -T -E -R is on it. That's another difference. It's, uh, it's, it's, it's not as I say, not as hard as, but few people would, uh, would, uh, bother with anything like that. The older ones, you know. They, they had hard times. That's all there's to that. I know people here in this town, they never met. You speak of it. Just as if it would be a disgrace. I don't, I'm proud. I'm proud of, of the country that we've come to.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, anyway, we, uh, from there on then I, uh, I worked out one summer here on the ridge, and I hadn't had time to be confirmed when I first got here because I, I was, uh, I had to work, you see, in them. So I went to, to confirmation school here. I'm not a Catholic. I'm Lutheran. And, uh, I don't see any difference in them myself. Lutherans or Catholic or all kinds of, I, I have often wondered why, uh, they have to be so many different kinds. And to, uh, that we can't, uh, agree for some concern, uh, it doesn't make any difference.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I think, they think I'm a heathen, you know. But, but, uh, I'm not. I, my sister Esther easily cried, you know, when she was a child.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, uh, one time she had spilled something, and she says, oh dear God, why did you let me, uh, spill that milk? And I was astonished to say, are you blaming God for it?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: But I had to laugh. That was funny, you know. She was just kind of scared, you know, to, to, oh, I think, I think that all of us would get to the right place. I don't, I don't, I don't worry about it.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Where was the Spring Valley School?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, when you leave Troy here and go to Derry, you have gone that way.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, the, the first main road that, what did you ask me?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Spring Valley Schoolhouse. The first, uh, big road to the left, coming from Troy, and the first road to the left was in a corner, the schoolhouse was in a corner, Spring Valley School. The main road went then, as it does now, towards Derry, and then here, there's road going towards Derry, and this one is the one that takes off, and that, uh, that divides here to go to the clear pit, and this one to that Spring Valley Lake, and then it comes to the same, to our place, uh, at the end of it when it comes together.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And, uh, what else about White Pine?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: That was all I asked. Oh, yeah. Well, they, they moved that and made a garage. I think, uh, I think that, Harry, Harry Glacier has bought that. Oh, yeah, that was an awful road then when we first came. It was terrible. It goes, walks so far, and often, we used, had salt in some lard, and you sat for butter if we didn't have anything else. But I wasn't alone. When I was in Sweden, I, I could remember many times, I, I was proud, you know, and we sat at a school room, uh, and the room where we had called it, uh, resting room, or something like that, they settled benches all around like this. Oh, a big room because they had, they had many children. Uh, you know, that's in Sweden. And, uh, the, that they'd eat, and they'd have apples, and they'd have corn candy. There were those I could, that have things then that I didn't have it. And then, so that they wouldn't make fun of me, or, or realize that I didn't chew it on anything, I just chewed with my hand, you know, and chewed, pretended to.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: But, uh, it has gone fine. It's so. Then, then I, um, I went to Pullman, and Dr. E .A.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Bryan, the president of the college, have I told you this?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Olivia was, happened, happened to get their place to work for Mrs. Bryan, and, uh, I had worked up on the American region, got enough to get a ticket to Pullman. So, uh, we, uh, we met, uh, we, we met Dr. Bryan, too, and they were very nice to us, and she told me afterwards, Olivia stayed there, I think, almost a year before she went to, to Great Falls, Montana. And, but, uh, Dr. Bryan said, don't you ever stop studying, don't ever stop, because he knew that I had books with me in my suitcase when I came, and everything. I had to, I had to go all the time. One time, he went to, he, he also was a Sunday school teacher. He, I don't know, what kind of a religion they had. He was a wonderful man, though.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: And he, um, he, he, he, I was, uh, studying, and he went through the room, the, the dining room, and he says, what are you reading?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I said, my lessons.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Who, who, who, where are you going to school?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: I'm not going to school right now.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Uh, well, what are you studying then?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: Well, I can read. I said, I, I, know what I'm, I'm doing. But, but he says, you, that's Latin. You can't, you can't, uh, have, have that. I certainly have, I said, and that, and that one was, uh, in Sweden. Uh, I had, um, Latin, and, and, uh, geometry. When I was 12 years old. But, uh, I don't remember much of it now. I think I did. They do if I wanted to. Uh, that's why I get so disgusted with some kind of kids. They, they, they just can't appreciate what they have. The, the books that they have, the art that they see, just kick at things and throw it away, and oh, that's nothing. That's wrong. It's wrong. Parents have to train them differently. I blame them more than anything else.

speaker: Laura Schrager or Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a hard time learning English?

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
words: No, no, nothing. Uh, there were so many Scandinavians in, in this part of the country at that time, an Irishman, you know, Irishman in 1992, I think, four, something like that, six. Uh, they, they, um, they, they are Italians. Yeah, they are Italians. Uh, more Italians, though, on that railroad than anyone else. Swedes went to the woods and, and, and filed homesteads, you see. Have homes, have homes. And, uh, they, there was a story about, uh, the name of Troy. Well, now then, they, they, let me find some paper someday. It's too long. But, but, uh, they, they wanted to have a name for Troy. Well, I know that the first, this come down from Moscow, Huff's Gulch, a fellow by the name of Huff, filed for a quarter section or something like that. But I don't think it was first, because, uh, before that, before I knew about that, why people talked about, um, uh, oh, run down to the, run down to the hotel, go, run down to the store and get the mail. Because, uh, they had, the mail went as far as, Troy had come then as far as Troy, you know, or Moscow, or they go horseback to Moscow to get the mail.

speaker: Anna Marie Anderson Oslund
